chunk_summary: Guidelines for assessing breastfeeding transfer and identifying supplementation triggers in preterm/LBW/ELBW infants post-discharge, focusing on intake, weight trends, and structured feeding assessments.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of breastfeeding transfer assessment in preterm infants post-discharge.
Identify triggers for supplementation based on weight gain and feeding patterns.
Learn structured methods for observing and quantifying breastfeeding effectiveness.
Apply breastfeeding-preserving strategies to support lactation and infant growth.
key_facts: Late preterm infants should feed at least 8 times per 24 hours; fewer feeds indicate inadequate intake.
Weight gain <15 g/day in late preterm infants is a trigger for supplementation.
Structured feeding assessments include feeding history, output integration, and latch/transfer observation.
Test weighing (pre/post feed weights) is supported for quantifying milk transfer when needed.
Supplementation should start with expressed mother’s milk, followed by formula if necessary.
Skilled lactation follow-up is recommended within 2–3 days after initiating supplementation.
reference_quote: Use a structured observed-feed assessment: take a feeding history, integrate output with weight trends, and observe latch and transfer using an attachment–transfer–endurance framework.
document_key_topics: Breastfeeding transfer assessment in preterm infants
Triggers for supplementation and weight monitoring
Structured feeding observation and test weighing
